content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 15, 1999

Craig J. Cannizzo
Hanson Bridgett Marcus Vlahos Rudy LLP
333 Market Street, 23rd Floor
San Francisco, California 94105-2173

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-99-212

Dear Mr. Cannizzo:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the Oak Valley Hospital District (the "Hospital") and its Chief Executive Officer, Dev Mahadevan, regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

1. Is Mr. Mahadevan a "public official" subject to the conflict of interest provisions of the Act?

2. If Mr. Mahadevan is a public official, how can he determine whether he has a conflict of interest in decisions on the Hospital's budget?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes, Mr. Mahadevan is a "public official" within the meaning of the Act.

2. Your account of the facts indicates that Hospital budget decisions may have a financial effect on St. Joseph's, which is a source of income to Mr. Mahadevan. To the extent that it is reasonably foreseeable that such decisions would have a material financial effect on St. Joseph's, Mr. Mahadevan would have a conflict of interest (within the meaning of the Act) in those decisions. We illustrate the analysis, and attach to this letter Commission information packets further explaining the Act's conflict of interest rules.

___________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-99-212
Page 2

FACTS

The Oak Valley Hospital District (the "District") is a local healthcare district organized
and operated under Health and Safety Code Sections 32000 et seq. The District owns and
operates the Oak Valley Hospital (the "Hospital"). St. Joseph's Regional Health System ("St.
Joseph's") is a nonprofit California corporation which has a management services agreement
with the District under which it provides management services for the Hospital.

Under the management services agreement, St. Joseph's is responsible for managing all
aspects of the Hospital's operations, including development of annual capital and operating
budgets and the provision of comprehensive financial management services. St. Joseph's
directly employs the Hospital's Chief Executive Officer, Dev Mahadevan, who in turn is
responsible for the overall, day-to-day management of the Hospital.² All departmental managers
at the Hospital report, directly or indirectly, to Mr. Mahadevan, who is accountable both to the
Hospital governing body and to the CEO of St. Joseph's. Although Mr. Mahadevan is employed
by St. Joseph's, his salary and benefits are charged to the Hospital as a monthly expense under
the management services agreement.

The Hospital's annual operating and capital budgets are developed in tandem, through a
process which begins from data provided by Hospital department managers. The managers'
funding requests are compiled by the Hospital's CFO and are then reviewed by Mr. Mahadevan,
with advice and counsel from Hospital administrative personnel. Mr. Mahadevan next
recommends a budget to the Hospital's Finance Committee, on which he has no vote. The
Finance Committee may question Hospital staff (including the CEO and CFO) regarding various
assumptions and projections used in developing the proposed budget. The Finance Committee
has, on occasion, modified one or more elements of the budget at this stage.

The Finance Committee's eventual recommendation is then presented to the Governing
Body of the Hospital, which has the ultimate authority to adopt the budget. The Hospital's
Governing Body consists of all five members of the District Board, plus two residents of the
District, two voting members of the Hospital's medical staff, and a representative from St.
Joseph's.³ A majority of the Governing Body can approve the budget, so long as two of the five
members of the District Board and the St. Joseph's representative agree. The Governing Body
itself may question Hospital staff on various items, and will occasionally modify one or more
elements of the budgets before final approval.

² Under the management agreement, St. Joseph's receives a management fee consisting of 1.25 percent of
the Hospital's annual net operating revenues and 25 percent of the Hospital's annual net income. St. Joseph's has
gross annual receipts of more than $100,000,000, but less than $400,000,000. The Hospital has annual receipts of
approximately $30,000,000, and net income of more than $2,000,000, but less than $3,000,000. Mr. Mahadevan's
income as Hospital CEO exceeds of $250 per year.

³ We presume that Mr. Mahadevan is not a member of the Governing Body.

File No. A-99-212
Page 3

ANALYSIS

1. Is Mr. Mahadevan a "public official" subject to the conflict of interest
provisions of the Act?

The conflict of interest provisions of the Act apply only to "public officials." A "public
official" is defined to include "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
government agency...." (Section 82048.) Since you understand Mr. Mahadevan to be an
employee of St. Joseph's, a non-profit corporation rather than a governmental agency, we must
first decide whether Mr. Mahadevan is a public official subject to the Act.

A local healthcare district organized and operated under Health and Safety Code Sections
32000 et seq. is a local governmental agency within the meaning of the Act, and indeed we have
reached that conclusion regarding the Oak Valley Hospital District specifically, in the Riddle
Advice Letter, No. A-97-294. The Hospital, owned and operated by the District, is likewise a
local governmental agency. It follows that an officer, employee or consultant of the Hospital is a
"public official." (Section 82048.)

Assuming for purposes of analysis that Mr. Mahadevan is neither an officer nor an
employee of the Hospital, he may still be a "consultant" within the meaning of the Act.⁴ We
have determined that employees of a nonprofit corporation may be classified as "consultants" if
they provide particular services to a governmental agency, pursuant to a contract between a
nonprofit corporation and a governmental agency. (See, e.g. Marks Advice Letter, No. A-98-
073.) Given a services contract obliging an individual to perform certain tasks for a
governmental agency, "consultant" is defined at Regulation 18701(a)(2), largely by specifying
responsibilities typical of a "public official:"

"Consultant means an individual who, pursuant to a contract with a
state or local government agency:
(A) Makes a governmental decision whether to:
1. Approve a rate, rule, or regulation;
2. Adopt or enforce a law;
3. Issue, deny, suspend, or revoke any permit, license, application,
certificate, approval, order, or similar authorization or entitlement;
4. Authorize the agency to enter into, modify, or renew a contract
provided it is the type of contract which requires agency approval;
5. Grant agency approval to a plan, design, report, study, or
similar item;

⁴ It is possible that Mr. Mahadevan might also be regarded as an official who manages public investments,
within the meaning of Section 87200. We need not resolve that question to provide the conflicts analysis you have
requested.

File No. A-99-212
Page 4

7. Adopt, or grant agency approval of, policies, standards, or
guidelines for the agency, or for any subdivision thereof; or
(B) Serves in a staff capacity with the agency and in that capacity
performs the same or substantially all the same duties for the
agency that would otherwise be performed by an individual hold-
ing a position specified in the agency's Conflict of Interest Code."

Since Mr. Mahadevan serves as the CEO of the Hospital, a local government agency, we
have little doubt that he is charged with some of the functions enumerated above and, at a
minimum, that he is "serving in a staff capacity" performing the duties of a CEO who, if
employed directly by the Hospital, would properly occupy a position specified in the agency's
Conflict of Interest Code. On the facts before us, we must conclude that Mr. Mahadevan is a
"consultant" to the Hospital, and therefore is a public official under Regulation 18701.

2. If Mr. Mahadevan is a public official, how can he determine whether he has a
conflict of interest in decisions on the Hospital's budget?

The Act's conflict of interest provisions help to insure that public officials perform their
duties impartially, free from bias attributable to their own financial interests or those of persons
who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public
official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.

A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the meaning
of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on
one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).)
The Commission has adopted an ordered process for determining whether the Act's conflict of
interest rules prohibit a given public official taking any role in a particular governmental
decision. (Regulation 18700(b).)

Since the Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to "public officials," the first
step is determining whether the subject of the inquiry is in fact a "public official." (Regulating
18700(b)(1).) Having answered that question in the affirmative, we next ask if the official will
be making, participating in making, or using his official position to influence, a governmental
decision. (Regulation 18700(b)(2).) Your account of the facts indicates that Mr. Mahadevan
will not be "making" the decision to adopt either the Hospital's operating or capital budgets.⁵
You are, however, especially interested in whether Mr. Mahadevan's role in the budget process
constitutes "participating in" or "influencing" these decisions. Regulations 18702.2 and 18702.3
define such conduct, subject to exceptions described at Regulation 18702.4, none of which
appear applicable in this case. We return then to Regulation 18702.2, which provides as follows:

⁵ Based on our assumption that he is not a member of the Governing Body, the board that actually votes to
adopt the budget.

File No. A-99-212
Page 5

"A public official 'participates in making a governmental
decision,' except as provided in Title 2, California Code of
Regulations, section 18702.4, when, acting within the authority of
his or her position, the official:
(a) Negotiates, without significant substantive review, with a
governmental entity or private person regarding a governmental
decision referenced in Title 2, California Code of Regulations,
section 18701(a)(2)(A).
(b) Advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker either
directly or without significant intervening substantive review, by:
(1) Conducting research or making any investigation which
requires the exercise of judgment on the part of the official and the
purpose of which is to influence a governmental decision
referenced in Title 2, California Code of Regulations, section
18701(a)(2)(A); or
(2) Preparing or presenting any report, analysis, or opinion, orally,
or in writing, which requires the exercise of judgment on the part
of the official and the purpose of which is to influence a
governmental decision referenced in Title 2, California Code of
Regulations, section 18701(a)(2)(A)."

Regulation 18702.3 provides:

"(a) With regard to a governmental decision which is within or
before an official's agency or an agency appointed by or subject to
the budgetary control of his or her agency, the official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official
contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any
member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency. Attempts
to influence include, but are not limited to, appearances or contacts
by the official on behalf of a business entity, client, or customer.
(b) With regard to a governmental decision which is within or
before an agency not covered by subsection (a), the official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official
acts or purports to act on behalf of, or as the representative of, his
or her agency to any member, officer, employee or consultant of an
agency. Such actions include, but are not limited to, the use of
official stationary."

The budgeting process you describe suggests that Mr. Mahadevan may participate in, or
use his official position to influence, decisionmaking on the Hospital budget. Although the
Governing Body is the decisionmaker, you indicate that the Governing Body may look to

the Act?
2. IfMr. Mahadevan is a public official, how can he determine whether he has a conflict
of interest in decisions on the Hospital’s budget?

CONCLUSION: 1. Yes, Mr. Mahadevan is a “public official” within the meaning ofthe Act.
2. Your account ofthe facts indicates that Hospital budget decisions may have a fmancial
effect on St. Joseph’s, which is a source of income to Mr. Mahadevan. To the extent that it is
reasonably foreseeable that such decisions would have a material financial effect on St. Joseph’s,
Mr. Mahadevan would have a conflict of interest (within the meaning of the Act) in those
decisions. We illustrate the analysis, and attach to this letter Commission information packets
further explaining the Act’s conflict of interest rules.
